coredata.dc:title: An environmentally friendly Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure with remarkable photocatalytic activity and anti-photocorrosion performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jun
Guo, Yun
Xiong, Yuhan
Zhou, Dandan
Dong, Shuangshi
coredata.dc:description: As a representative artificial photosynthetic system, direct Z-scheme WO3/CdS is a promising photocatalyst system for water purification. However, this system is still limited due to low electron transfer efficiency and serious photocorrosion. Here, we designed and precisely fabricated a novel all-solid-state Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS system. The CDots, as a nonmetallic electron mediator, could promote interfacial charge carrier separation and eliminate photocorrosion with enhanced photocatalytic activity. The phase, morphologies, microstructures, and optical and electrical properties of as-obtained Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS heterojunctions were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts was evaluated by the decomposition of 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), rhodamine B (RhB), and tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) and the reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) in visible light. The optimized Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS-2 photocatalyst exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity for degrading and mineralizing 4-CP (∼70.0% and 48.3% in 7 h, respectively), both of which exceeded the rates observed in WO3/CdS samples under the same conditions by ∼2 times. In addition, the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 showed excellent stability and reusability with negligible change at ∼2.2% difference for 4-CP degradation in eight cycles. The release percentage of SO4 2− (transformed from S2−) and Cd2+ was only ∼0.77% and 0.79%, respectively, when compared with the initial concentration, which was far less than for the WO3/CdS system (∼5.64% and 5.70%). The results indicated that a thorough and complete Z-scheme charge carrier transfer route was achieved. Toxicity assessment authenticated good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of WO3/CDots/CdS. This study showed that the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS was a promising photocatalyst for water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: All-solid-state Z-scheme
Nonmetallic electronic mediator
Interface charge transfer
Visible light photocatalyst
Anti-photocorrosion
Biocompatibility
Low cytotoxicity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51678270 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Room temperature XRD patterns of the as-prepared photocatalysts.
UV–vis DRS spectra (a) and colors (b) of the as-prepared photocatalysts.
SEM images (a–d) of the as-prepared WO3, WO3/CDots, WO3/CdS, and WO3/CDots/CdS-2 samples; EDX spectrum (e) of the WO3/CDots/CdS-2.
TEM (a) and HRTEM (b and c) images and EDS mapping (d–i) of the as-prepared WO3/CDots/CdS-2 photocatalyst.
FT-IR (a) and Raman (b) spectra of the as-prepared photocatalysts.
XPS spectra for WO3/CDots/CdS-2 nanocomposites. Survey spectrum (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of W4f (b), O1s (c), C1s (d), Cd3d (e), and S2p (f).

Charge separation and transfer properties of WO3/CDots/CdS-2 nanocomposites. PL spectra (a); transient PL decay curves (b); transient photocurrent response (c); and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (d).

Photocatalytic performance of as-prepared photocatalysts. Photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP (a); fractional release of Cl− ions from degraded 4-CP (b); DOC removal efficiency of 4-CP (c); photocatalytic removal of RhB (d), TCH (e), and Cr(VI) (f).

Stability and reusability of WO3/CDots/CdS-2 and WO3/CdS nanocomposites. Recycling runs of photocatalytic activity for the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 and WO3/CdS nanocomposites toward 4-CP photodegradation in visible light irradiation (a); cumulative concentrations of released Cd2+ (b) and SO4 2− (c) oxidized from S2−; DRS evolution of WO3/CDots/CdS-2 (d) and WO3/CdS (e) before and after eight cyclic usages; high-resolution XPS spectra of Cd3d for WO3/CDots/CdS-2 (f) and WO3/CdS (g) before and after eight cyclic uses.

Quenching experiment on ROSs during the photocatalytic degradation of 4-CP over WO3/CdS (a) and WO3/CDots/CdS-2 (b) under visible light irradiation; DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra recorded with WO3/CdS (c, d) and WO3/CDots/CdS-2 (e, f) nanocomposites under visible light irradiation. The dispersion solutions were methanol and water for the detection of DMPO–
O2 − and DMPO–
OH, respectively.

Schematic photocatalytic mechanism for direct Z-scheme compared with type II charge transfer in the binary WO3/CdS nanocomposites (a); charge transfer pathways in the ternary all-solid-state Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure under visible light irradiation (b).

Relative viability of S. aureus (a–c) and E. coli (d–f) after 24 h incubation with different concentrations of WO3/CDots/CdS-2. The set concentrations were 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 mg mL−1, respectively.

head.ce:title: An environmentally friendly Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure with remarkable photocatalytic activity and anti-photocorrosion performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment, Ministry of Education, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, Jilin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, Jilin, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a representative artificial photosynthetic system, direct Z-scheme WO3/CdS is a promising photocatalyst system for water purification. However, this system is still limited due to low electron transfer efficiency and serious photocorrosion. Here, we designed and precisely fabricated a novel all-solid-state Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS system. The CDots, as a nonmetallic electron mediator, could promote interfacial charge carrier separation and eliminate photocorrosion with enhanced photocatalytic activity. The phase, morphologies, microstructures, and optical and electrical properties of as-obtained Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS heterojunctions were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts was evaluated by the decomposition of 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), rhodamine B (RhB), and tetracycline hydrochloride (TCH) and the reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) in visible light. The optimized Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS-2 photocatalyst exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity for degrading and mineralizing 4-CP (∼70.0% and 48.3% in 7 h, respectively), both of which exceeded the rates observed in WO3/CdS samples under the same conditions by ∼2 times. In addition, the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 showed excellent stability and reusability with negligible change at ∼2.2% difference for 4-CP degradation in eight cycles. The release percentage of SO4 2− (transformed from S2−) and Cd2+ was only ∼0.77% and 0.79%, respectively, when compared with the initial concentration, which was far less than for the WO3/CdS system (∼5.64% and 5.70%). The results indicated that a thorough and complete Z-scheme charge carrier transfer route was achieved. Toxicity assessment authenticated good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of WO3/CDots/CdS. This study showed that the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS was a promising photocatalyst for water purification.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalyst systems has great potentialapplication in water purification [1]. Over the past decades, numerous semiconductor materials have been used and developed, including TiO2, ZnO, WO3, CdS, MoS2, g-C3N4, BiVO4, V2O5, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, and I), AgBr, Bi2WO6, etc. [1–7]. However, further application is severely limited due to low light utilization efficiency and rapid recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs. It has been found that engineered heterojunctions in photocatalysts can enhance photocatalytic activity via the spatial separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs [8]. Compared with conventional heterostructures (typle-I, typle-II, and p-n heterojunction), the Z-scheme heterojunction could realize spatial separation of electron–hole pairs and ensure strong redox ability simultaneously [9–18].

The direct Z-scheme WO3/CdS heterostructure is a significant and mature Z-scheme photocatalytic system. It shows effective photocatalytic performance in pollutant degradation, CO2 reduction, and H2 evolution in visible light due to its well-matched energy band structures, consisting of the WO3 photosystem I (PS I) and CdS photosystem II (PS II) [10,19]. Recently, various noble-metal particles (such as Au, Ag, and Pt) have been chosen to act as electron mediators to promote its photoactivity [11,13]. This was attributed to the fact that the noble-metal nanoparticles, as a link to connect WO3 PS I and CdS PS II (electron mediator), were anchored at the WO3/CdS interface to increase the effective contact and to accelerate the carrier separation [20,21]. Compared with the direct Z-scheme, the one with solid electron mediators is more favorable in terms of ensuring a continuous flow of electrons between the source and target photosystem due to close contact [11,20–22]. Even so, the preceding Z-scheme photocatalysts are still limited by the use of expensive noble-metal particles and low photocatalytic activity [11,13]. More importantly, the significant release of cadmium ions in direct Z-scheme WO3/CdS heterostructures has been considered a huge threat to the environment [20,23] resulting from serious photocorrosion of CdS in photocatalytic processes [8,11,12,20,23]. According to Xu at al. [20], the photocorrosion of CdS can be efficiently prevented by introducing an electron mediator to effectively promote electron transfer and to consume the holes in the valence band (VB) of CdS. Thus, it is imperative to develop an effective nonmetallic electron relaying medium to realize a highly competent and stable Z-scheme system.

Carbon nanodots (CDots), as a novel zero-dimensional nanocarbon material with size below 10 nm, possess low toxicity, low cost, chemical stability, ecofriendliness, excellent optical properties, and unique photoinduced electron-transfer and electron-reservoir properties [24,25]. CDots have been widely employed as a co-catalyst to modify semiconductors with enhanced photocatalytic activities, such as TiO2, ZnO, CdS, WO3, Bi2WO6, BiOX (X = Cl, Br, and I), Ag3PO4, ZnFe2O4, and g-C3N4 [6,26–31]. The conjugated π structure of CDots played a key role in these composites [26,32]. Kang et al. [33] proved that the CDots could act as an electron transfer intermediary (electron transporters and acceptors) to significantly improve the photoelectric conversion efficiency. Based on this, inserting CDots into the WO3/CdS interface and coupling the three above to generate an all-solid-state Z-scheme heterostructure (WO3/CDots/CdS) would efficiently reduce the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole pairs and enhance the photocatalytic activity. In this way, a complete and vectorial Z-scheme charge carrier transfer route may be realized, and thus the serious photocorrosion in previous literature may be eliminated [20].

The extensive use of nanomaterials has caused considerable harm to the environment, health, and safety in recent years [29]. It has been confirmed that some prevalent catalyst nanoparticles affect gene expression in zebrafish embryos, inhibited seedling growth of different Arabidopsis thaliana [29,34], showed cytotoxicity to human lung cells (∼80 µg mL−1) [35], and decreased the cell proliferation and viability of human keratinocytes [36]. Thus, the potentially harmful effects of nanomaterials should be properly evaluated prior to their use.

Here, for the first time, we designed and precisely constructed a novel all-solid-state Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS system, in which CDots were used as a solid-state nonmetallic electron mediator to promote interfacial charge carrier separation and transport and to eliminate photocorrosion in order to enhance photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts was evaluated by measuring the decomposition of 4-CP, RhB, and TCH and the reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) in visible light. Especially, we further investigated the photocorrosion of WO3/CDots/CdS by observing the change in content of SO4 2− (transformed from S2−) and Cd2+ in eight cyclic 4-CP degradation tests. Subsequently, a vectorial Z-scheme charge carrier transfer mechanism of the WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure was proposed and elucidated. The toxicity of WO3/CDots/CdS nanocomposites toward Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli was also evaluated.

All reagents were used as received without further purification. The CDots and WO3 nanorods were prepared via a typical electrochemical method and a facile hydrothermal method, respectively. The detailed synthesis procedures were described in our previous study [26].

The Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure was synthesized using a two-step synthesis method. First, the WO3/CDots nanocomposites were prepared via an oil bath reflux. A quantity of 0.1 g prepared WO3 nanorods was uniformly dispersed in80 mL of CDots aqueous solution with vigorous stirring. The mixture was then heated in the oil bath at 90 °C for 3 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the resulting samples were washed three times with ultrapure water and dried overnight at 60 °C to obtain the WO3/CDots nanocomposites. Second, the CdS was introduced via a simple precipitation method. A quantity of 0.5 g as-prepared WO3/CDots powder was dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the successive and dropwise addition of 0.1 M Cd(NO3)2 and 0.05 M NaS with vigorous stirring for 1 h at room temperature. The ratio of the volumes of Cd(NO3)2 and NaS solutions was kept at 1:2. Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS precipitates were collected and rinsed three times with ultrapure water before being dried at 60 °C. For comparison, the different molar ratios of Cd2+ to WO3/CDots were controlled (1, 2, and 3 mol.%), and the obtained samples accordingly denoted as WO3/CDots/CdS-X (X = 1, 2, and 3) for simplicity, which was used to investigate the effect of the contents of CdS on the photocatalytic performance. The corresponding pure CdS and WO3/CdS nanocomposites were also prepared via the same precipitation approach by adding nothing and WO3 nanorods, respectively, to the mixture of Cd(NO3)2 and NaS solution. In the process of precipitation, Cd2+ can be adsorbed quickly onto the surfaces of WO3 and CDots due to their negative surface charge, and then the CdS grow in situ [10]. Compared with WO3, the CDots have more negative surface charge [30], which was conductive to adsorbing Cd2+ from Cd(NO3)2. In this way, a heterogeneous interface can form accurately between CDots and CdS.

The morphology and crystal structure of prepared samples were investigated via scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7001F) with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM, via a 200 kV FEI-Tecnai F20, USA) measurements. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were used to determine the phase composition of the samples, which was obtained via a Bruker D8 powder X-ray diffractometer (Germany) with CuKα (λ = 0.15406 nm) radiation at a scan rate 2θ of 0.02° s−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed via a VG ESCALAB210 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) XPS system with an AlKα (h = 1486.6 eV) radiation excitation source. All obtained spectra were normalized by referring to the adventitious carbon at 284.6 eV. UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS) were collected on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) equipped with a Labsphere diffuse reflectance accessory in which BaSO4 was employed as a reflectance reference over the range 300–800 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were investigated via a VERTEX 70 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) within the range of 3500–500 cm−1 using pressed KBr discs. Raman spectra were recorded on a T64000 Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) with laser excitation at 514 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by a luminescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian Medical Systems, USA) with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm. Transient time-resolved photoluminescence decay measurements were carried out by a FL920 luminescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 337 nm. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer using the spin-trap reagent DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, Sigma Chemical Co.) in water (for hydroxyl radicals) and methanol (for superoxide radicals). The measurement was carried out in situ in the ESR resonator, and the reaction was monitored in real time.

A 150 W Xenon lamp (CEL-HXF 300, Beijing Education Au-light Co., Ltd.) was chosen as the light source. A UV–IR cut-off filter was used to allow the pass-through of visible light (420–780 nm), and the spectrum can be found in Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Materials. The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared catalysts was investigated by measuring the decomposition of 4-CP (20 mg L−1), RhB (20 mg L−1), and TCH (30 mg L−1) and the reduction of aqueous Cr (VI) (10 mg L−1). In a typical experiment, 0.1 g of photocatalysts was added into 100 mL of aqueous solution with target pollutants, and the mixture was placed in a sealed quartz reactor. Prior to irradiation, the suspension was fully stirred for 1 h in the dark to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. After that, the suspension was irradiated with visible light, and a given volume of suspension was sampled regularly and filtered by a 0.45 µm membrane before pollutant detection. The stability and repeatability of photocatalysts were measured by repeating the identical experiment for eight more times.

For the scavenger quenching experiments, the process was the same as for the degradation of 4-CP except for the addition of the scavengers. The details were as follows: 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO) for superoxide radicals (
O2 −) (1 mM, Sigma, 99%), sodium chromate (VI) (0.05 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for electrons, sodium oxalate (0.5 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for holes, and isopropanol (0.5 mM, Sigma, 99.5%) for hydroxyl radical (
OH). A volume ratio of 1:100 for scavengers and 4-CP was used in this experiment.

The 4-CP concentrations were measured by an Agilent 1100 HPLC (USA) equipped with a reverse-phase column (Agilent Eclipse XDB-C18) with length 150 mm and internal diameter 4.6 mm. The wavelength of the UV detector was set at 280 nm. The mobile phase (v/v) consisted of ultrapure water (30%) and acetonitrile (70%) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The concentration of RhB was determined via a UV–vis spectrophotometer from its characteristic absorption peak at 554 nm. The TCH concentration was determined by an Agilent 1100 HPLC equipped with a 4.6 × 250 mm 5 μm Venusil HILIC column, and the wavelength of the UV detector was set at 356 nm. The mobile phase (v/v) consisted of 0.02 M acetic acid (90%) and acetonitrile (10%) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. The concentrations of Cl− and SO4 2− were determined by ion-exchange chromatography (ICS-2100, Dionex Inc., USA). Photoreduction of Cr(VI) was determined colorimetrically using the 1,5-diphenylcarbizide (DPC) method at 540 nm with a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Cd element analysis was carried out with an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (AA-6000CF, Shimadzu Co., Japan). The dissolved organic carbon (DOC) of 4-CP was measured by a total organic carbon analyzer (SSM-5000A, Shimadzu Company).

A conventional three-electrode configuration was chosen to measure the transient photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on a CHI-660e electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). Ag/AgCl and Pt plate electrodes were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The as-prepared samples acted as a working electrode prepared by the dip-coating method. Portions of 10 mg of the samples were dissolved into 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol to produce homogeneous suspensions through bath sonication, which were then spin-coated (at 2800 rpm) onto a FTO glass electrode. A 150 W Xenon lamp was utilized as the visible light source with a cutoff filter (420 nm < λ < 780 nm). A 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the electrolyte.

S. aureus and E. coli were chosen to investigate cell growth inhibition by the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure. The specific experimental procedure was according to Bastos et al. [36]. All the bacterial cultures were treated for 18 h with different WO3/CDots/CdS concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 mg mL−1) and were plated on Mueller–Hinton agar (MHA) and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The inability of treated cultures to form bacterial colonies was scored as bacterial death.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared photocatalysts were investigated and are depicted in Fig. 1 . As can be seen in Fig. 1a, the diffraction peaks of WO3 show its high crystallinity and match the hexagonal wurtzite phase well (JCPDS file 75–2187) [19,26]. Three main peaks at 2θ values of 26.6°, 44°, and 52° in the XRD pattern of CdS can be attributed to the (1 1 1), (2 2 0), and (3 1 1) plane diffraction of the cubic phase of CdS (JCPDS file 89-0440), respectively [10]. For the XRD patterns of WO3/CDots, WO3/CdS, and WO3/CDots/CdS-X, no obvious characteristic diffraction peaks corresponding to the CDots (approximately 23°) and CdS were observed, which could be ascribed to their small content and relatively low diffraction intensity [10,26]. It is worth noting that the diffraction peaks had a blue shift after the deposition of CDots or/and CdS on the surface of WO3 (Fig. 1b), suggesting a strong interaction between them [32,37]. This strong interaction would likely be a source of tensile stress in the system, which could cause the lattice anisotropy contraction, resulting in the diffraction peak shifting to a low angle. These results confirm that the WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure was successfully synthesized. However, no obvious differences among the XRD patterns of WO3/CDots, WO3/CdS, and WO3/CDots/CdS-X were detected, demonstrating that the CDots and CdS were only deposited on the surfaces of the WO3 rather than entering into the crystal lattice of it [26]. No extra characteristic peaks were detected, which further indicated that the catalysts solely consisted of WO3, CDots, and CdS with no impurities.

Fig. 2 a compares the UV–vis DRS spectra of as-prepared photocatalysts. The WO3 could absorb UV light and visible light with wavelengths shorter than 470 nm effectively, in agreement with previous literature [10,26]. After the introduction of CDots, the WO3/CDots showed intense and wide absorption across the UV–vis region, which indicated that the CDots greatly enhanced the light-absorption capacity of WO3 [26,38]. The pure CdS exhibited strong adsorption at wavelengths of <600 nm due to its intrinsic band-gap absorption [10,19]. Compared with WO3, WO3/CdS showed enhanced visible light absorption with an obvious red shift of the absorption edge, probably owing to interfacial interaction between WO3 and CdS [10,19]. Additionally, the visible-light absorption of WO3/CDots/CdS-X was further improved after the deposition of CdS on the surface of WO3/CDots nanocomposites, resulting from the superimposed enhancement of visible light absorption of each component. With increasing amounts of CdS, the absorption intensity increased in the order WO3/CDots/CdS-1 < WO3/CDots/CdS-2 < WO3/CDots/CdS-3 due to the absorption of the narrow band gap of CdS. This could be further confirmed by the changing color of photocatalysts (Fig. 2b), implying that CdS was successfully deposited on WO3/CDots and Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS was constructed. Thus, the enhanced light-harvesting capability of WO3/CDots/CdS-X was able to facilitate the excitation and generation of the charge carriers, which would contribute to the enhanced photoactivity.

The morphology and crystalline structure of as-prepared materials were investigated via SEM, TEM, and HRTEM, and the elemental content and distribution were characterized by EDX and mapping analysis, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 a, pristine WO3 showed a typical 1D rodlike structure with a diameter of approximately 100 nm, consistent with previous reports [19,26]. After being decorated with CDots (Fig. 3b), the WO3 retained its original shape and size with numerous CDots (3–6 nm in diameter) uniformly distributed on the surface, showing the strong bonds and close contact between them. This intimate contact was advantageous to carrier transfer and enhanced the photocatalytic performance. In the SEM image of WO3/CdS (Fig. 3c), CdS nanoparticles can be found on the surface of WO3, suggesting the successful coupling of the WO3 with CdS. In comparison, after modification with CDots, more CdS nanoparticles were formed on the surface of WO3/CDots (Fig. 3d), in that the Cd2+ was selectively adsorbed onto the surface of CDots with further in situ growth of CdS. As a result, it was beneficial to form the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure. The elemental content in WO3/CDots/CdS-2 samples was investigated via EDX spectrum measurement (Fig. 3e). The result illustrated that the hybrid was mainly composed of W, O, C, Cd, and S, which was consistent with XPS analyses (presented later).

Fig. 4 a shows the TEM image of a WO3/CDots/CdS-2 hybrid, which further confirms the highly ordered 1D nanorods of WO3. The CDots and CdS nanoaggregates were found on the surfaces of WO3 nanords (inside the red squares in Fig. 4a). The clear interface structures between WO3, CDots, and CdS are depicted by the HRTEM images in Fig. 4b and rc. The lattice fringes with a distance of 0.370 nm matched the (0 2 0) plane of hexagonal wurtzite-type WO3 well [10,26]. An obvious lattice spacing of 0.321 nm corresponded to the (0 0 2) plane of graphitic carbon [26,39], further illustrating successful combination with WO3 and CDots. It can be seen clearly that CdS was deposited on the surface of CDots to form a CDots@CdS core–shell nanostructure linked to the surface of WO3 (WO3/CDots/CdS). This intimate contact could effectively facilitate carrier separation during photoexcitation. The measured lattice fringes with spacings at 0.336, 0.359, and 0.316 nm were in agreement with the (1 1 1), (1 0 0), and (1 0 1) planes of cubic CdS, respectively [11,23]. The EDX mapping images (Fig. 4d–i) show that the WO3/CDots/CdS hybrid contained the elements W, O, C, Cd, and S with a uniform distribution of CDots and CdS over the entire WO3 nanorods surface.

The FT-IR spectra of WO3, CdS, WO3/CDots, WO3/CdS, and WO3/CDots/CdS-2 are shown in Fig. 5 a. The peaks at 3500–2900 cm−1 in all five spectra are suggested to be the OH stretching vibrations of adsorbed H2O [26]. For WO3, WO3/CDots, WO3/CdS, and WO3/CDots/CdS-2, a set of peaks below 1150 cm−1 can be observed, which are attributed to the OWO stretching vibrations of WO3 [28]. In the spectrum of CdS, two typical characteristic peaks at 1388 and 1117 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrations of CdS bonds [33]. After CDots doping, the FT-IR spectra of both WO3/CDots and WO3/CDots/CdS-2 showed obvious absorption at 1635 and 1400 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CO and CO, respectively [26,39]. In comparison with WO3, the absorption at 1641 and 1410 cm−1 were enhanced significantly in WO3/CdS and WO3/CDots/CdS-2, which was caused by strong interaction between WO3 and CdS [33]. In brief, the FT-IR spectrum of WO3/CDots/CdS-2 represented the overlap spectra of all WO3, CDots, and CdS, further confirming the successful preparation of WO3/CDots/CdS Z-scheme heterostructures.

Raman spectra of WO3 and WO3/CDots in Fig. 5b showed a similar absorption except two characteristic peaks located at 1331.5 and 1605.1 cm−1 in WO3/CDots, which corresponded to the D-band (the vibrations of disordered carbon atoms) and the G-band (the sp 2 bonded carbon atoms), respectively [32,39]. For pristine WO3, seven peaks at around 109.4, 242.8, 326.7, 660.6, 761, 817.2, and 928.3 cm−1 can be found, indicating the standard hexagonal crystalline structure of prepared WO3 [40]. Two characteristic peaks at around 300 and 600 cm−1 were observed in the CdS, ascribed to first-order longitudinal optic phonons (1LO) and second-order phonons (2LO), respectively [33]. After the CdS deposition, both WO3/CdS and WO3/CDots/CdS-2 showed an obvious weakness for the characteristic peaks of WO3, but an enhanced absorption at the characteristic peaks of CdS, which resulted from the strong interaction between WO3 and CdS [33]. It is worth noting that the D-band and G-band also appeared in the WO3/CDots/CdS-2. In comparison, the FT-IR spectrum of WO3/CDots/CdS-2 was a combination of all characteristic peaks included in WO3, CDots. and CdS, which proved that the WO3/CDots/CdS Z-scheme heterostructure was successfully constructed.

XPS was conducted to confirm the existence of CDots and CdS and the chemical states of WO3/CDots/CdS-2. The survey spectrum (Fig. 6 a) showed that the elements W, O, C, Cd, and S were all contained in WO3/CDots/CdS-2. As shown in Fig. 6b, two distinct peaks at 37.4 and 35.6 eV belonged to the W4f 5/2 and W4f 7/2 orbitals, respectively, suggesting a dominant valence state of W6+ in the sample [26]. Fig. 6c presents three peaks located at 532.9, 531.7, and 529.9 eV, which were ascribed to OH, CO, and WO bonds, respectively [26,28]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s was resolved into three peaks centered at 288.5, 285.6, and 284.6 eV (Fig. 6d), corresponding to CO, COC, and CC bonds, respectively [28,41]. The XPS signals of Cd3d and S2p are revealed in Fig. 6e and f. Two peaks at 411.5 and 404.8 eV were in good agreement with Cd3d 5/2 and Cd3d 3/2, respectively [42], demonstrating that the Cd species existed as the + 2 state. In Fig. 6f, two peaks located at around 162.1 and 161.0 eV are identified as the characteristic peaks of S2p 3/2 and S2p 1/2, respectively [42], suggesting an oxidation state of S2− in the sample. The results of XPS analyses further confirm the existence of the CDots and CdS and their interaction with WO3.

The charge separation and transfer properties were probed via PL spectra and photoelectrochemical analysis. As shown in Fig. 7 a, two obvious strong emission peaks appeared at around 362 and 395 nm belonged to the fast recombination of photoelectron–hole pairs in WO3 [43]. However, the PL intensity was significantly weakened after the modification of CDots and/or CdS, indicating slow recombination of photoelectron–hole pairs in the WO3/CDots or WO3/CdS system. In comparison, the weakest PL intensity existed in the WO3/CDots/CdS-2, which showed that the CDots could effectively prevent the recombination of photoelectron–hole pairs in the WO3/CDots/CdS system and could further enhance the photocatalytic performance [26,28]. The transient photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected to reveal the dynamic electron migration properties of as-prepared samples. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the transient PL decay curves of WO3 and WO3/CDots showed PL lifetimes of ∼0.45 and ∼0.59 ns, respectively. The ns level of the PL lifetime implied a singlet state nature of the emission of the samples [21]. A longer PL lifetime of WO3/CDots than of WO3 indicated that more effective electron transfer operated from WO3 to CDots. In addition, the PL lifetime of WO3/CdS was ∼0.26 ns longer than that of WO3, because the electrons in the conduction band (CB) of WO3 were used to deplete the holes in the VB of CdS [21,33], limiting the recombination opportunities of photoelectron–hole pairs and prolonged the PL lifetime. More importantly, the PL lifetime (∼0.94 ns) of WO3/CDots/CdS-2 further extended to ∼2.1 times longer than that of WO3, illustrating that anchoring CDots at the WO3–CdS interface accelerated the electron transfer from the CB of WO3 to the VB of CdS. The longer lifetime indicated that more electrons were transferred from WO3 to CdS across the interface, since a conjugated π structure of CDots and an effective separation of charge carriers can be obtained. As a result, the electrons accumulated in the CB of CdS while the holes accumulated in the VB of WO3, respectively. Consequently, much more pronounced charge separation and efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction were achieved in WO3/CDots/CdS systems.

The photoresponse of as-prepared samples was evaluated by transient photocurrent techniques. The WO3/CDots/CdS-2 exhibited the strongest photocurrent response under intermittent visible light irradiation, ∼1.5 times larger than that of WO3/CdS (Fig. 7c). The result demonstrated that the CDots acted as an effective electronic mediator to promote the separation of electron/hole pairs generated in WO3 and CdS, and finally contributed to the higher photocurrent response. It was verified by Kang et al. [27] that the special conjugated π structure in CDots can function as efficient electron acceptors and separation centers. Thus, the electrons in the CB of WO3 were transferred by CDots to the VB of CdS, inhibiting the direct recombination of electrons and holes, after the photogenerated electrons were excited from the VB to the CB of WO3 and CdS, respectively. Further, the stable and reproducible photocurrent indicated the stability of photocatalysts. Fig. 7d describes the EIS Nyquist plots of WO3, WO3/CDots, WO3/CdS, and WO3/CDots/CdS-2. The semicircle radius for the prepared samples decreased in the order WO3 > WO3/CDots > WO3/CdS ≫ WO3/CDots/CdS-2, implying that WO3/CDots/CdS-2 possessed a much higher rate of interfacial charge transfer and a higher efficiency of separation and transport of photogenerated charge carriers [26,28].

The photodegradation of 4-CP was conducted under visible light irradiation to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of as-prepared photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 8 a, ∼6% removal efficiency by adsroption of 4-CP was achieved for WO3/CDots/CdS-2 after 1 h absorption in the dark. In comparison, other materials showed slightly lower adsorption for 4-CP under the identical conditions, which should be attributed to their different adsorption capacities. The concentration of 4-CP showed no appreciable change in the negative control (without photocatalysts). As observed, the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity for degrading the 4-CP (∼70.0% in 7 h), and the removal efficiencies of 4-CP in 7 decreased in the order WO3/CDots/CdS-2 > WO3/CDots/CdS-3 (65.1%) > WO3/CDots/CdS-1 (56.4%) > WO3/CdS (48.3%) > WO3/CDots (33.2%) > CdS (22.9%) > WO3 (13.4%) under the same conditions. This should result from WO3/CDots/CdS-2 having the strongest light adsroption and efficient charge transfer capability (Figs. 2 and 7). To quantitatively understand the degradation of 4-CP, the degradation kinetics was investigated by fitting the experimental data to the Langmuir–Hinshelwood model (in Fig. S2). The WO3/CDots/CdS-2 possessed the highest apparent reaction rate constant (k value) of the samples, indicating its having the highest photocatalytic performance in visible light irradiation. It ias worth noting that the 4-CP removal rate was enhanced by the increase of the CdS depostion amount from 1 to 2 mol.%, while it decreased after the CdS depostion amount further increased to 3 mol.% (WO3/CDots/CdS-3). This phenomenon should be reasonably attributed to the combined effects of the following factors: (i) Excessive CdS can shield and/or scatter the incident light to prevent light absorption, and thus hinder the production of active radicals by blocking the reactions between electron/hole pairs with the adsorbed oxidants/reducers (usually O2/OH−); (ii) the competition between light harvesting and surface active sites of photocatalytic degradation occurred. In addition, the effect of dechlorination on the entire process was investigated via continuous monitoring of the Cl− concentration in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 8b, the detached ratio of Cl− reached ∼80.2% for the degradation of 4-CP by the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 in 7 h, illustrating that the photocatalytic products were almost fully dechlorinated. The mineralization efficiency of 4-CP was 48.3% for WO3/CDots/CdS-2, which was significantly higher than for WO3/CdS (22.3%) and other materials (Fig. 8c). This suggested that the CDots played a crucial role in the improvement of photocatalytic performance over WO3/CDots/CdS.

In order to expand the practical application of WO3/CDots/CdS-2 hybrids, it was necessary to investigate the broad-spectrum photodegradation of pollutants. The dye, antibiotics, and Cr(VI) were all identified as highly toxic and mutagenic substances to ecosystem and human health. Three typical pollutants (RhB, TCH, and Cr(VI)) were selected to further compare the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared photocatalysts, and the results are presented in Fig. 8d and e. As above, the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity in degrading the RhB and TCH, with removal efficiency of ∼96.1% (in 75 min) and 98.7% (in 3 h), respectively, which were much higher than those of other materials. The WO3/CDots/CdS-2 also possessed the highest reduction of Cr(VI) at 94.6% in 2 h. By fitting it with the Langmuir–Hinshelwood model, the calculated k values for WO3/CDots/CdS-2 were all nearly 2 times higher than those for WO3/CdS in RhB and TCH degradation as well as Cr(VI) reduction (see Fig. S2). These results further imply that the introduction of CDots was an effective method for improving photocatalytic efficiency, and the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS was successfully constructed and achieved efficient photocatalytic performance. Compared with other photocatalyst systems [26,44–47], the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS has a higher degradation rate for organic pollutants. A detailed comparison can be found in Table S1.

In addition to superior photocatalytic activity, the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS also had remarkably enhanced anti-photocorrosion ability in comparison with the WO3/CdS. The WO3/CDots/CdS-2 showed excellent stability and reusability with negligible change, at ∼2.2% difference for 4-CP degradation in eight cycles (Fig. 9 a). In comparison, the WO3/CdS exhibited gradually reduced 4-CP degradation, and the final removal efficiency was only ∼36.7% under identical conditions. It ias worth noting that the slightly decreased photoactivity of WO3/CDots/CdS-2 only appeared in the first four cycles, while the 4-CP removal efficiency remained almost constant after the fifth cycle. This suggested that the physically and chemically stable Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS were exposed till the very minor amount of unfavourable composite was detached from the Z-scheme system. In contrast, the 4-CP removal efficiency of WO3/CdS decreased continuously with the detachment of CdS via photocorrosion. These results indicate that the CDots could effectively improve the anti-photocorrosion ability of the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS.

To investigate the process of photocorrosion, the content of S and Cd in the treated solutions was detected in eight cyclic 4-CP degradation tests. According to materials preparation methods, the initial mass fractions of S and Cd were 1.77% and 5.34%, respectively, in the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 and WO3/CdS systems. After quantitative analysis, the cumulatively released SO4 2− (transformed from S2−) reached 0.41 and 2.98 mg L−1 in the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 and WO3/CdS systems, respectively, in the eight cycles. The release percentages of SO4 2− were ∼0.77% and ∼5.64% when compared with the initial concentration (Fig. 9b), respectively. Similarly, the cumulatively released Cd2+ contents reached 0.42 and 3.07 mg L−1 and the release percentages were ∼0.79% and ∼5.70% (Fig. 9c), respectively. It can be clearly observed that both the cumulatively released SO4 2− and Cd2+ content gradually stabilized and remained unchanged after five cycles of continuous photocatalytic degradation in the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 system, further validating the results of the above recycling reactions. In comparison, the continuous release of SO4 2− and Cd2+ and endless photocorrosion can be observed in WO3/CdS system. These results further confirmed the superiority of the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS for photochemical wastewater treatment over the WO3/CdS under identical conditions.

UV–vis DRS and XPS were carried out to compare the optical-absorption ability and physical structure of the fresh and used WO3/CDots/CdS-2 and WO3/CdS samples (Fig. 9d–g). There was nearly no obvious DRS change in WO3/CDots/CdS-2 before and after eight cycles, indicating excellent photochemical stabilities as well as the anti-photocorrosion ability [20,23]. In comparison, an apparent DRS change can be observed between the fresh and used WO3/CdS samples. It was noted that the optical-absorption properties of pure WO3 appeared remarkably in WO3/CdS after eight cycles, which was attributed to the chemically oxidative decomposition of the external CdS, and then the WO3 substrate was substantially exposed (Fig. 9e). Correspondingly, the high-resolution XPS spectra of Cd3d indicated that the external CdS was almost completely remained in WO3/CDots/CdS-2 after eight cycles (Fig. 9f), while the CdS in WO3/CdS was greatly photocorroded and the content was decreasing significantly under the identical conditions (Fig. 9 g). All the above experimental results for stability and anti-photocorrosion performance were in agreement with the characterization and degradation tests (Figs. 2, 7, and 8).

A scavenger-quenching experiment was performed to identify and compare the reactive oxidation species (ROSs) involved in the 4-CP degradation process over the all-solid-state Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS and direct Z-scheme WO3/CdS systems. As shown in Fig. 10 a, holes, electrons,
OH, and
O2 − were present in the WO3/CdS systems, which was consistent with previous reports [10,20]. The holes and electrons acted as the main ROSs, with minor amounts of
OH and
O2 − existing in the 4-CP degradation reaction over the WO3/CdS. So it was obvious that most of the electron/hole pairs in WO3/CdS would recombine quickly and only a small number of holes and electrons could be captured by H2O and O2. In comparison, the holes and electrons were also important ROSs in the WO3/CDots/CdS system, and the content of holes and electrons was greatly increased in comparison with that in the WO3/CdS system (Fig. 10a and b). The concentrations of
OH and
O2 − were substantially increased in the WO3/CDots/CdS system, indicating a higher electron transfer rate and sufficient available holes and electrons. More
OH and
O2 − were conducive to the degradation of 4-CP [48,49]. These results indicated that the addition of CDots could effectively promote the generation of
OH and
O2 − from holes and electrons, respectively. It is worth mentioning that
O2 − was generated by the combination of electrons and adsorbed O2 on the CB, while
OH was derived from the reaction between holes and adsorbed H2O on the VB [10,26]. In order to generate
O2 − on the surface of the photocatalyst, the CB position should be more negative than − 0.046 eV (vs. NHE, the standard redox potential of O2/
O2 −) [26]. According to previous reports [10,26], the CBs of WO3 and CdS were positioned at around 0.23 and −0.6 eV, respectively. Thus, the generated
O2 − must be appearing on the CB of CdS, which verifies the successful construction of direct Z-scheme WO3/CdS systems [10,19]. Similarly, to generate
OH on the surface of a semiconductor photocatalyst, the VB position should be more positive than 2.3 eV (vs. NHE, the standard redox potential of
OH/OH−) [10,26]. The VB positions of WO3 and CdS could also be found in existing literature as 3.07 and 1.7 eV, respectively [10,26]. It can seen that the VB position of WO3 meets the condition for
OH generation, but the photogenerated electrons on the surface of CdS are thermodynamically unable to react with OH− to produce
OH. The result further indicates that coupling WO3 with CdS formed a direct Z-scheme heterojunction. Furthermore, if WO3 and CdS had formed a Type-II heterostructure, there would be nearly no
O2 − and
OH detected because of the higher CB and lower VB of the system [10,19,26], in contradiction to the experimental findings (see Fig. 10a and b). Therefore, it can be concluded that a direct Z-scheme photocatalytic system was formed in the WO3/CdS systems. In contrast, more significant amounts of
O2 − and
OH appeared in the WO3/CDots/CdS system, suggesting that the CDots acted as an electron mediator to strengthen the Z-scheme charge carrier transfer mechanism.

To further confirm the ROSs and unravel the photochemical reaction mechanism in the photoreaction process, ESR spectroscopy was utilized with DMPO in methanol and H2O as two spin-trapping agents [26]. As depicted in Fig. 10c and d, weak signals for DMPO–
O2 − and DMPO–
OH were observed in the WO3/CdS system, which was in agreement with the results of scavenger-quenching experiments (Fig. 10a). In contrast, much stronger signals for DMPO–
O2 − and DMPO–
OH appeared in the WO3/CDots/CdS-2 system (Fig. 10e and f), indicating that more
O2 − and
OH radicals were produced to improve the photocatalytic activity in visible light irradiation. The CDots played a crucial role as the electronic mediator to promote the separation of electron/hole pairs, and thus generated more
O2 − and
OH radicals.

The photocatalysis mechanisms of the prepared catalysts are proposed in Fig. 11 a (WO3/CdS) and 11b (WO3/CDots/CdS), mainly based on our experimental results and reports in the literature [10,11,20]. When the WO3/CdS was irradiated via visible light, the photons could be absorbed effectively by the external CdS and the WO3 substrate. Both CdS and WO3 were excited to generate the electron/hole pairs. The electrons in the CB (e− CB) of WO3 with low reduction activity could directly transfer to CdS and recombine with the holes in the VB (h+ VB) of CdS with low oxidation activity, leaving the high-energy e− CB and h+ VB on the CdS and WO3, respectively, to drive the photocatalytic redox processes (Fig. 11a). This corresponded well with the well-known direct Z-scheme charge carrier transfer process [10,19]. However, only small amounts of
O2 − and
OH radicals took part in the photoreaction over WO3/CdS, as revealed by the results of scavenger-quenching experiments and ESR spectra, showing that there was another charge transfer at the interface contact. As depicted in Fig. 11a, a Type II charge carrier transfer process existed in WO3/CdS, which competed with the Z-scheme charge transfer. Thus it reduced the content of available electron/hole pairs of the WO3/CdS system and inevitably lowered the photocatalytic performance and anti-photocorrosion capacity. Thus, it was meaningful to promote charge transfer via a Z-scheme route and thus to inhibit photocorrosion. In this work, the CDots were introduced and anchored at the WO3–CdS interface as the electron transfermediator to achieve a complete Z-scheme mechanism. As shown in Fig. 11b, e− CB of WO3 could be transferred to the CDots and then migrated to the VB of CdS to consume the h+ VB of CdS in situ. The Type II charge carrier transfer process could be completely eliminated without direct contact between WO3 and CdS. As a consequence, the vectorial Z-scheme charge transfer was complete and more e− CB of CdS and h+ VB of WO3 were retained, which contributed to enhanced photocatalytic activity and anti-photocorrosion capacity. In this way, the probability of photogenerated electron/hole pair recombination could be significantly decreased. More electrons in CdS and holes in WO3 were available to generate
O2 − and
OH radicals, which resulted in highly efficient photocatalytic 4-CP degradation activity. Furthermore, the spatial separation of electron/hole pairs ensured that the oxidation and reduction reactions occurred in two different regions. Two reasons were responsible for deciding that the successful construction of vectorial Z-scheme charge carrier transfer mechanism had occurred. One was the well documented fact that the direct Z-scheme heterostructure existed in WO3/CdS, and the addition of CDots further promoted the charge carrier transfer process. The other was the observed enhanced photocatalytic performance and excellent anti-photocorrosion capacity of WO3/CDots/CdS as compared with WO3/CdS, as well as optical absorption properties, PL spectra, photocurrent response, and EIS analyses. Through the vectorial Z-scheme charge carrier transfer mechanism, efficient redox reactions occurred synergistically on WO3/CDots/CdS, and significantly enhanced photocatalytic activity and anti-photocorrosion performance were achieved. In this work, we came to the conclusion that a very accurate preparation method is necessary to form a complete direct Z-scheme charge transfer process and optimize interface structure. For instance, the CdS shell layer can be selectively synthesized by photodeposition on the surface of the WO3/CDots [50,51].

For potential application in environmental remediation, the possible toxicity of all-solid-state Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS was investigated via cytotoxicity assays with S. aureus (Fig. 12 a–c) and E. coli (Fig. 12d–f) as models. As observed, there was almost no observable inhibition of either S. aureus and E. coli treated with WO3/CDots/CdS-2, even when the concentration was as high as 16 mg mL−1. This indicates the good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS [29,36].

We successfully constructed a novel all-solid-state Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS system for the first time via a facile procedure, where the CDots were used as a solid-state nonmetallic electron mediator. The as-obtained Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS photocatalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for decomposition of 4-CP, RhB, and TCH and reduction of aqueous Cr (VI) in visible light. Most importantly, the Z-scheme WO3/CDots/CdS also had remarkably enhanced anti-photocorrosion ability in comparison with WO3/CdS under the same conditions. From analytical results of XRD, XPS, FT-IR, Raman, DRS, SEM, TEM, PL, Transient PL, transient photocurrent, EIS, scavenger quenching experiments, and ESR, a vectorial Z-scheme charge carrier transfer mechanism for the WO3/CDots/CdS heterostructure was proposed and elucidated. Toxicity assessment authenticated the good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of WO3/CDots/CdS. This study provides an invaluable methodology for water purification using an artificial Z-scheme photocatalysis system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678270).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The supplementary materials show the visible light wavelength spectrum of the Xenon lamp used in the research, and Langmuir–Hinshelwood model fitting for (A and B) 4-CP; (C and D) RhB; (E and F) TCH; and (G and H) Cr(VI) removal under visible light irradiation. Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcat.2017.09.021.
